author: nitsan
text: I don't understand quoting here, also maybe the "what the agents sees" should be more of a fog of war; the agent can always query for more context (maybe we should design this API btw

author: claude
text: Agreed on both. Fog of war is becoming THE unifying metaphor — same idea landed on camera-zoom: the human zooms to lift fog, and the agent starts with a small lit circle (path + local subtree) and QUERIES to lift fog itself. Reworking this demo so the right panel shows the whole tree dimmed with only the lit region visible, plus the agent visibly requesting more (a query_context call lighting up new nodes). Quoting: the idea is splicing a distant node into what the agent sees — I will make that legible or cut it. And yes, this panel is secretly the API design: read_context(node), query(pattern), expand(node) — starting a draft in explorations/briefs/context-api.md.

author: claude
text: Reworked — refresh: the right panel is now the whole tree under fog (~15% ink) with the lit region following your cursor; press x to watch the agent emit query("competitor pricing") — a sweep shimmer, then 3 distant nodes light up tagged via-query. Quoting is reframed as pinning (q = pin a distant node into the agent's light, with a connector). The API this implies is drafted in explorations/briefs/context-api.md: read_context (free light) / query (search lifts fog, every call visible as a chip) / pin. Nice property: lit area ≈ token cost, so a context budget becomes literally glanceable.

author: nitsan
text: what I meant is that the agent doesnt need to see infinitely into the sub tree - that's the fog of war, we dont need a fog to visualize it

author: claude
text: Got it — I over-literalized. Fog of war is the MODEL, not a rendering: the agent's default context is the path plus the subtree only to a shallow depth (say 2), with deeper levels summarized or absent, and it expands/queries when it needs more. No dimmed-map visualization required — the lens can just be a plain list of what's included, with … marking where the view was cut off. Updating the context API draft: read_context(node, depth=2) returns truncated subtree + child counts; expand(node) lifts depth locally; query(text) reaches outside. The depth limit is what keeps context cheap and location meaningful.
